data.title: Hong Kong-backed ‘Lion Rock’ chip debuts in boost to city’s semiconductor standing
data.description:
A Hong Kong-invested chip start-up on Friday debuted a processor named after the city’s landmark mountain Lion Rock, a development that is set to elevate the city’s standing in the semiconductor landscape.
The RISC-V-based data centre chip from Shanghai semiconductor start-up StarFive – which received an undisclosed investment from the Hong Kong Investment Corporation (HKIC) in March – aims to take advantage of surging demand for computing power for artificial intelligence applications.
The company had received orders for the chip and would soon start mass production, it said on Friday.
StarFive founder and CEO Thomas Xu Tao said at a launch event in Hong Kong that the chip, which uses the RISC-V open-source architecture, was “independent and controllable”.
“Our objective right now is very clear: to make RISC-V a reality in the age of AI and to bring China’s chip innovations to the world,” he said.
The chip was compatible with Intel servers, and xFusion, a spin-off from Huawei Technologies, was among its first group of clients, the company said. XFusion was previously the x86 server unit under Huawei.
RISC-V, pronounced “risk five”, refers to the fifth generation of the Reduced Instruction Set Computer, an open-source central processing unit architecture that allows developers to configure their integrated circuit designs.
The technology competes with Intel’s x86 and Arm Holdings’ self-named architecture, which dominate, respectively, the personal computer and smartphone markets. It is still far less popular owing to its current performance limits.
Chinese businesses and the government in recent years have increased their efforts to develop RISC-V chips, hoping that the technology would help the country reduce its reliance on foreign suppliers amid tightened US export controls on advanced chip technology.
Hong Kong has also pledged to develop its RISC-V industry in a bid to secure a position in China’s semiconductor landscape and drive economic growth.
The investment in StarFive by HKIC, the government’s investment vehicle, is seen as a sign of the city’s confidence in open-source standards and its ambition to support the mainland semiconductor industry’s development.
StarFive built up a presence in the city before the investment. It launched a RISC-V processor in collaboration with the Hong Kong and China Gas Company (Towngas) in November 2022 for use in smart gas metres.
The company had supplied 6 million of the chips so far, it said on Friday.
StarFive was considering pursuing an initial public offering (IPO) in Hong Kong through the Hong Kong stock exchange’s Chapter 18C listing rules, said Alan Chan, managing partner of Full Vision Capital, StarFive’s founding investor.
Xu did not disclose a planned timeline for its IPO, but said that it would happen “soon”.
Chapter 18C, put in place in 2024, relaxed eligibility rules including revenue requirements to make it easier for specialist technology companies to carry out share offerings.

data.title: China hits third 1,000-tonne gold belt this year that holds ‘all treasures’
data.description:
China has discovered a rare gold deposit in the Kunlun Mountains near the western border of Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region, according to government geologists involved in the survey.
Initial estimates suggest its total gold reserves could exceed 1,000 tonnes.
“The outline of a thousand-tonne-scale gold belt in West Kunlun, Xinjiang, is now taking shape,” wrote He Fubao, a senior engineer with the Kashgar Geological Team, and his colleagues in a paper published on November 4 in the peer-reviewed journal Acta Geoscientica Sinica.
This discovery marks the third gold deposit with the potential to surpass the 1,000-tonne threshold disclosed by Chinese authorities in less than a year, following major finds in Liaoning province in the northeast and Hunan province in central China.
Before these announcements, the world’s largest known gold deposits typically held only a few hundred tonnes. The industry had estimated that only about 3,000 tonnes of gold remained unmined in China, just a quarter of the remaining untapped gold in Russia and Australia.
The rapid succession of these new discoveries suggests China’s gold reserves could be much bigger than previously thought.
This surge in discovery is attributed to a significant increase in exploration spending and the deployment of advanced technology. Chinese geologists have developed a suite of cutting-edge tools, including artificial intelligence, the world’s most powerful ground-penetrating radar systems and highly sensitive mineral exploration satellites.
The impact of these new technologies has gone beyond the Chinese border, helping to identify new gold deposits in many other regions such as West and Central Africa.


In ancient Chinese mythology, the Kunlun Mountains are revered as a sacred, divine range, often compared with Mount Olympus in Greek mythology. According to the ancient text “The Classic of Mountains and Seas”, Kunlun was considered the centre of the world and a repository of “all treasures on Earth”.
Over 2,100 years ago, Emperor Wu of the Han dynasty, impressed by a massive dragon-shaped mountain range in southwestern Xinjiang, officially named it “Kunlun”, a designation that persists today.
Modern science has confirmed that the geographical centre of the Eurasian continent is located in Xinjiang, and the region is rich in valuable minerals such as jade. However, until now, the ancient legends of Kunlun being rich in gold lacked scientific evidence.
In recent years, Chinese scientists have found promising signs of large gold deposits in the western segment of the Kunlun range, near the border with Kyrgyzstan. The most exciting find is the Kuokejilega deposit.
This site was first noted by the Kashgar Geological Team of the Xinjiang Bureau of Geology in the early 1990s but was quickly abandoned because the surface exposure was too small. Exploration was revived in 2019, following breakthroughs in China’s deep-earth prospecting technology.
The project has since yielded a series of major discoveries.
The gold at Kuokejilega is embedded in a colossal, 100km (62-mile) long scar – a massive “ductile-brittle shear zone” – where the Earth’s crust has been violently twisted and ripped apart over hundreds of millions of years, according to He’s team.
Ancient rivers of superheated water, loaded with gold, surged through these deep cracks, leaving behind a trail of riches in the form of quartz veins and “alteration halos” within a specific layer of 400-million-year-old rock. The ore itself is hidden as “sparse, speckled” dustings of toxic minerals such as arsenopyrite and pyrite within dark, slate-like rock.

Geologists have defined 87 gold-bearing bodies in the area, with six being the main orebodies, according to He and his colleagues.
The concentration of mineralisation is currently mapped in the upper 300 metres (984 feet) of the crust, making it relatively accessible for mining. The average gold concentration, or grade, is above the minimum level required for profitable extraction.
The team estimates the resource potential far exceeds the “large” category, placing it in the “super-large” prospect range.
“The Kuokejilega gold deposit is the first large-scale gold find in the western segment of the West Kunlun metallogenic belt in recent years. Its discovery fills a gap in the region’s geology, which previously lacked evidence of large gold deposits, and reveals enormous potential for further gold exploration,” He’s team wrote.
“Together with a series of medium-to-large gold mines discovered in succession, such as Tugenmansu and Bashiganke,” the total reserves in West Kunlun could exceed 1,000 tonnes, they added.
In 2018, China built a massive, cross-shaped antenna array with a surface area exceeding the size of New York City five times over. This facility sends powerful, low-frequency electromagnetic waves that can penetrate deep into the Earth’s crust and travel across China and its surrounding regions.
This allows scientists to map subsurface geology and identify buried mineral bodies at depths of several kilometres with unprecedented precision, a capability that no other nation currently possesses.
Leveraging this facility and other powerful scientific instruments, Chinese researchers have made potentially textbook-changing breakthroughs not only in gold but also in the exploration of critical resources such as lithium, uranium, rare metals, oil and natural gas, significantly strengthening China’s position in global supply chains.

data.title: The best things to do in Hong Kong this week, November 16-22
data.description:
Eat this
Mandarin Grill + Bar

The Mandarin Oriental’s long-standing steakhouse welcomes new head chef Junior Nadje, who has cooked at some of France’s most prestigious kitchens including Alléno Paris, La Mère Brazier and La Chèvre d’Or. Get to know his grill-centric culinary style with the four-course Discovery Menu, featuring dishes such as grilled mullet with shiso purée and tarragon-infused grilled chicken.
1/F, Mandarin Oriental Hong Kong, 5 Connaught Road Central
Drink this
Dragonfly

Tucked in Tai Kwun’s historic former superintendent’s house, this fantastical cocktail lounge has been dazzling guests since 2018 with its ceiling of turquoise stained-glass lamps and creative concoctions. This month, the bar has unveiled a fresh menu inspired by the golden age of mixology, turning classic cocktails from the United States, Britain, Mexico and the Caribbean into modern gems. Highlights include Legacy, a zesty tequila, mescal and grapefruit blend, and the Muzo Emerald, an alcohol-free take on the Moscow Mule served in a bohemian, art nouveau-inspired dreamscape.
Shop 10-G1, Tai Kwun, 10 Hollywood Road, Central
See this
Freespace Dance

The West Kowloon Cultural District’s annual dance festival returns for a third year from November 20 to December 7 under the theme of “Speculative Futures: Asia-Pacific Perspectives”. Featuring artists from Hong Kong, Japan, Australia, Korea and beyond, the festival dives into the future of dance with bold, cross-disciplinary collaborations. Expect immersive, augmented-reality dances by groups such as Chunky Move from Melbourne and Jungle from Seoul, plus platforms for artistic exchange and engaging fringe activities.
Freespace, West Kowloon Cultural District, 8 Austin Road West, Kowloon. For tickets, go to westk.hk
Don’t miss
Victoria Harbour Race 2025

Thousands will once again take the plunge for one of Hong Kong’s most thrilling sporting events, the Victoria Harbour Race. This year, the event is scheduled for November 22 and will feature 4,000 swimmers tackling a 1km course from Wan Chai’s Golden Bauhinia Square to Tsim Sha Tsui’s Avenue of Stars. A new Companion Team option lets friends and family swim together. After a 2024 hiatus due to the threat of a typhoon, the beloved Hong Kong tradition is back in all its glory, along with free viewing for spectators.
For details, visit hkharbourrace.com

data.title: Why a document on South Korea-US trade, security pacts is sparking concerns
data.description:
South Korea and the United States have released a long-awaited joint document detailing their recent trade and security agreements, including Washington’s approval for Seoul to build nuclear-powered submarines.
However, analysts warn that key gaps in the fact sheet could lead to both governments interpreting their partnership very differently, particularly on sensitive defence provisions and the feasibility of South Korea’s submarine ambitions.
The publication comes two weeks after President Lee Jae-myung and US President Donald Trump signed a sweeping set of agreements on the sidelines of the Asia-Pacific Economic Cooperation summit in South Korea.
The delay in producing the document reflects the challenges both sides faced in reconciling their differences and agreeing on the precise terms during the talks, according to analysts.
“The Korea–US trade, tariff and security negotiations, which were among the biggest variables for our economy and security, have now been conclusively wrapped up,” Lee said on Friday.
“With this agreement, the alliance has further evolved into a future-oriented, comprehensive strategic alliance encompassing security, the economy and advanced technology.”
He called the outcome “a meaningful achievement” that was secured through “common sense and reason”.

Under the agreement, Seoul confirmed a US$350 billion investment pledge, including US$150 billion earmarked for the US shipbuilding industry, in exchange for Washington lowering tariffs on Korean goods from 25 per cent to 15 per cent.
The document also outlines a series of new security arrangements, including US approval for South Korea to build nuclear-powered attack submarines and to reprocess spent nuclear fuel for commercial use.
Lee said that the investment commitments would remain within “levels our economy can fully sustain” and be directed only towards commercially viable projects.
His remarks appeared aimed at countering domestic criticism that Seoul’s financial pledges amounted to thinly disguised subsidies for US industries. “Concerns that Korea might effectively be providing free grants to the United States have now been dispelled,” he said.
Kang Jun-young, a professor at the Graduate School of International Studies at Hankuk University of Foreign Studies, agreed that the document helped clarify the broad outlines of what Lee and Trump agreed on.
It highlights an expansion of the bilateral cooperation from shipbuilding, nuclear energy and artificial intelligence to semiconductors, Kang said.
The most politically charged element of the document concerns US approval for South Korea to pursue nuclear-powered submarines – a long-standing national ambition that successive South Korean presidents have sought but failed to achieve.
The fact sheet leaves a lot of room for different readings
Doo Jin-ho, Korea Research Institute for National Strategy’s senior researcher
However, the document leaves a critical issue unresolved: the manufacturing location for the submarines.
Seoul insists construction must take place in South Korea, while Trump has suggested that Korean engineers would work at a shipyard in Philadelphia recently acquired by South Korean conglomerate Hanwha Group.
The Philadelphia facility, however, lacks the infrastructure to build nuclear-powered submarines, and analysts say any upgrading towards such a goal would require significant time and investment.
South Korean National Security Adviser Wi Sung-lac stated Seoul’s position, saying: “The discussions between the two leaders were conducted on the premise from beginning to end that the submarines would be built in Korea. The option of constructing our nuclear-powered submarine in the US was never mentioned.”
But analysts warn that the ambiguities in the document could lead to contrasting interpretations in the two countries.
“Particularly in the defence sector, the fact sheet leaves a lot of room for different readings,” said Doo Jin-ho, a senior researcher at the Korea Research Institute for National Strategy.
“It’s anyone’s guess when the submarines could actually be launched. There are so many prerequisites, including amending the bilateral civil nuclear energy agreement – that must come first.”
Beijing has voiced alarm over South Korea’s submarine plan.


China’s ambassador to South Korea, Dai Bing, said on Thursday that the move raised issues “directly linked to the global non-proliferation regime as well as stability on the Korean peninsula and in the broader region”.
According to a report by South Korea’s Yonhap news agency, Dai told reporters: “We hope the Korean side fully considers the concerns of all parties and handles this issue prudently.”
Dai also criticised Washington’s efforts to broaden the scope of the alliance between Seoul and Washington beyond deterring North Korea to containing China.
“The Korea–US alliance is historically rooted, and we respect that,” he said. “But if the strategic objectives of the alliance change, China’s perspective on the alliance could also change.”
Referring to speculation that US forces in South Korea could be mobilised in a Taiwan contingency, he warned: “If there are new developments related to this, China will express grave concerns.”
Dai said the “One China” principle – which states that Taiwan is part of China – was explicitly recognised in the documents establishing diplomatic ties between Seoul and Beijing.
“China will not tolerate any external interference in its domestic issue of Taiwan. We hope the Korea–US alliance will never play with fire when it comes to the Taiwan question.”

data.title: Hong Kong needs a new breed of lawmaker to steer its future course
data.description:
As 161 finalised hopefuls compete in Hong Kong’s second “patriots-only” Legislative Council elections, they will have just a few weeks to make themselves and their policy platforms known to the wider public. Some with political backgrounds already have exposure, but it may be limited to the district level.
Of the candidates, 49 are district councillors, a high number that is more than three times the 14 in 2021. Thirty-two of them are competing in direct or geographical elections. Name recognition will be all the more important as few have the star quality of Vivian Kong Man-wai, the Olympic fencing gold medallist, who is eyeing the functional seat for tourism.
All 161 candidates have qualified because they are considered patriotic and love Hong Kong. That is another way to spell out “one country two systems”, with “country” being considered first and foremost.
It’s how the reformed electoral system is meant to function, as Hong Kong moves from Western-style confrontational politics towards a consensus-building democracy aligned with the city’s social and political realities.
Many of the best-known old guards are retiring from Legco. Any political body needs new blood and talent, so this ought to be welcome, even if some new lawmakers will have to prove their mettle.
A new breed of lawmakers must be critical in a constructive way, strive to deliver results, be flexible in dealing with the government and responsive to their constituents’ needs.


It won’t be easy. They need to find a sweet spot between cooperation and checks and balances, so there is trust and confidence among officials, even when they or their departments are being criticised. They must understand that it isn’t personal, nor is it political theatre, as it was in the past before the electoral reforms.
Beijing has framed Hong Kong’s transformation as going from chaos to order. Whether you agree or not, the central government’s confidence in the city’s political system is sine qua non of its support and promotion of Hong Kong’s status as the nation’s premier financial centre.
Meanwhile, the global environment is undergoing unprecedented changes and presenting unpredictable challenges that require new thinking and governance. We need new talent in Legco who combine international perspectives with local experience and understanding.
That vision is incorporated into Hong Kong’s inclusion in the national 15th five-year plan as the city embraces key projects such as the Northern Metropolis to further integrate the Greater Bay Area.

But, in their day-to-day work, lawmakers must realise that ordinary people by and large care most about livelihood issues, from housing and education to employment and healthcare. Those with experience in the district councils have been exposed to grass-roots concerns, and it is heartening that the first two election forums saw candidates focusing on such issues. In the end, whether district or legislative, councillors are there to serve people and represent their interests.
The December election will be more intense than the last one. The number of candidates for direct election has reportedly increased by 45.7 per cent compared with the previous election, averaging more than five candidates for every two seats in each constituency. The contests for functional constituencies and Election Committee seats will be just as fierce, if not more so.
The reformed elections have been denigrated by the Western media and their governments, claiming that Hong Kong is being moulded into mainland China’s image. Well, how can it be otherwise? Should Hong Kong be allowed to evolve into something like an American city, a British city or an Australian city? That is surely absurd.
No one expects New York, London or Sydney to be like a Chinese city, even though their operations and development could well improve by borrowing some aspects of Chinese-style governance.
Before the electoral reforms, many Hongkongers were said to be afraid of Beijing’s authoritarian interference and fought against integration with their own country, even though it has been the biggest economic success story in human history and an engine of global growth. They were told by the now-defunct opposition and its Western backers that their own country was a monster.
It turns out there is nothing to be scared of, as Hongkongers who make millions of trips across the border every year to shop, wine and dine, and also to live, can testify.
Hong Kong is reaching the next stage of the nation’s development, and this election will be crucial to producing future political leaders who will steer the course for us.

data.title: Exhausted and forgiven Hong Kong striker aiming to avoid Singapore tears
data.description:
A jet-lagged and exhausted Michael Udebuluzor has vowed to be fit and firing for Hong Kong’s “do-or-die” Asian Cup qualifying clash with Singapore next Tuesday.
Striker Udebuluzor, who plays for Estonian top-flight side JK Tallinna Kalev, was replaced after 55 minutes of the 1-1 friendly draw with Cambodia on Thursday after he complained about breathing problems.
The 21-year-old was back in the squad following his exclusion from September and October get-togethers as punishment for calling Chinese supporters b******* after Hong Kong’s 1-0 East Asian finals defeat by China in July.
“I love coming back to Hong Kong. It feels good to wear the jersey again and I want to give everything,” Udebuluzor said. “I tried [against Cambodia], but I didn’t feel great, and I couldn’t breathe properly.
“I arrived on Sunday night and have barely slept. I’m still jet-lagged and exhausted. By Tuesday, my system will have started functioning properly.”
Udebuluzor acknowledged that the game with Singapore at a sold-out Kai Tak Stadium would be one of the biggest of his career.
The equation is simple: A win for Hong Kong would book their spot in the 2027 finals. Lose and Singapore go to Saudi Arabia instead. Only a draw would send the qualification race to the final round of matches next March.

“If we win, we qualify; if we lose, we cry, and I don’t want to cry,” Udebuluzor said.
“I want to play because I want to win for Hong Kong. There could be zero people watching, and I’d want to win.
“But, hopefully, we can use the 50,000 fans to our advantage. They will push us and make the other team nervous.”
Udebuluzor won the first of his 20 caps in September 2023, shortly after obtaining his passport.
He said he had not been surprised by the mushrooming interest in the city team in the intervening years, because “they told me the project before I came”.
Although only 7,480 showed up at unloved Hong Kong Stadium for the Cambodia fixture, that was more than the 5,289 who filed into the venue to watch Hong Kong beat the same opponents last October.
“If you win games, everything follows, and we’ve won a lot with the previous coach [Jorn Andersen], and now with Ashley [Westwood, current boss],” Udebuluzor said.
“Everything that’s happened is deserved. The city is with us and, hopefully, we will give back by winning on Tuesday. I think Hong Kong fans are the best in the world; I have never seen any other place like it.”

Westwood, meanwhile, absolved Udebuluzor of any blame for his enforced withdrawal, given he had played on Saturday in Estonia and travelled immediately afterwards.
Nonetheless, the Englishman said it was frustrating that “we don’t have lots of players who can play at the intensity we want for 90 minutes”.
“We’re narrowing down the players we can really rely on,” Westwood said.
“The last thing you want is to have to make substitutions for physical reasons. It’s always an issue at this level: you never know what players are doing every day at their clubs.
“You can’t gather enough information or data. That’s the job and you have to factor it into your decision making.”
With Oliver Gerbig suspended for Singapore, Shinichi Chan has emerged as the leading candidate to fill the hybrid defender-midfielder position.
“Shinichi is tall, fast, comfortable on the ball and a good defender,” Westwood said.
Westwood will choose between Tse Ka-Wing and 41-year-old Wang Zhenpeng in goal.
“It’s a flip of a coin; we have to trust one of them and, hopefully, it’s the right one,” Westwood said.

data.title: US-China tensions offer Asean ‘transformative’ critical minerals opportunity: economist
data.description:
Southeast Asian economies have a “transformative” opportunity to enter the world’s critical mineral supply chain amid China-US trade tensions, a senior Asian economist said.
Critical minerals – essential raw materials for the production of hi-tech gadgets, cars and aerospace equipment – will make the 11-member Association of Southeast Asian Nations’ (Asean) supply chain “more resilient and sustainable”, said Yasuto Watanabe, director of the Asean+3 Macroeconomic Research Office (AMRO).
The office was set up by the finance ministers of the Asean member states and the “plus three” – China, Japan and South Korea – following an agreement in 2009.
“Several member economies possess notable reserves and are actively exploring ways to integrate more deeply into the fast-growing clean-technology and advanced-manufacturing ecosystems,” Watanabe told the Post on Wednesday.
“Viewed through an ‘Asean plus three’ lens, the opportunity is transformative,” he said, with the Southeast Asian economies contributing resources and their “strategic location” to the critical minerals supply chain, and the other economies offering capital and technology.
In October, the United States signed memorandums of understanding with Asean members Malaysia and Thailand to secure critical minerals, namely the 17-member subset known as rare earth elements. These minerals are in particularly high demand for their utility in crafting permanent magnets and other technologically demanding components.
Malaysia already has processing capacity, setting it apart from other countries that just have reserves. Developing a mine from scratch to export capacity can take up to a decade, analysts have said.
The region must overcome … huge upfront costs in early-stage exploration
Deepali Bhargava, ING
The role of critical minerals in global supply chains received widespread attention this year when China – which accounts for about 70 per cent of global rare earth mining and 90 per cent of processing output, according to the Centre for Strategic and International Studies think tank – expanded the scope of its export controls on the materials.
The move alarmed Washington, hastening its search for alternative sources as it seeks to mitigate Beijing’s near-monopoly status, a source of leverage for China in the two countries’ prolonged trade war.
While Southeast Asian countries had a chance to produce some rare earth metals, given their extensive deposits, they would find it hard to “catch up” with China due to a lack of processing technology, said Liang Yan, a professor of economics at Willamette University in the US.
China might end up providing that technology, she said.
Beijing’s 12-year-old Belt and Road Initiative, which is focused on building trade-linked infrastructure abroad, was now helping Southeast Asian countries with “financing models” and technology, Watanabe said, adding that Beijing was pursuing infrastructure projects that “align more closely” with the needs of the region and its 700 million people.
This financing might come in the form of low-interest project loans, Liang said.
Deepali Bhargava, head of research and chief economist for Asia-Pacific with Dutch financial services firm ING, said access to funding would be essential for the region to make a difference in the minerals trade.
“To play a significant role in global [critical mineral] supply chains, the region must overcome … huge upfront costs in early-stage exploration and the need for funding across the entire value chain with a focus on sustaining autonomy and regional coordination.”

data.title: Anxiety to admiration: a day as a triathlon trainee with Hong Kong’s top coach
data.description:
While I maintain a regular functional fitness routine, let’s be honest – swimming, cycling and running have never been my strong suits.
So when the chance came up to train for one of the toughest sports – triathlon – with an experienced coach right here in Hong Kong, I felt nervously excited.
The opportunity presented itself last week when I met Andrew Wright, head coach of the Triathlon Association of Hong Kong, China, who has been training the city’s elite triathletes for the 15th National Games.
The squad representing Hong Kong – one of the host cities of the Games this year – includes Oscar Coggins, Robin Elg, Wong Tsz-to, Bailee Brown, Cade Wright and Hilda Choi Yan-yin. They will compete in individual races and the mixed relay at Central Harbourfront and Victoria Harbour on Saturday and Sunday.
I initially approached Andrew just asking for an amateur training plan – something I could quietly do in my own time.
But he took it a huge step further.
He offered to personally design a special session for me at the Hong Kong Sports Institute in Sha Tin, where the city’s best triathletes train, giving me a genuine, hands-on taste of elite sports conditioning.
I immediately accepted the challenge, though I knew there was no way I could complete a National Games-level triathlon.
Honestly, just thinking about the distances gives me anxiety.

The elite athletes are aiming for the podium in the main individual event, which comprises a gruelling 1,500-metre (4,921-feet) swim, a 40km (25 miles) bike ride and a 10km run.
My reality check: I’m not a strong swimmer, and finishing a 10km run alone is already a struggle. But getting a glimpse into the dedication and discipline required for such a high-stakes event was an opportunity I could not turn down.
Andrew explained that a full-time, national-level triathlete trained for about 30 hours a week – a regime that included roughly 25 to 30km of swimming, 10 to 14 hours of cycling, and 70 to 90km of running, plus a couple of gym sessions for muscle conditioning to prevent injury.
It’s a staggering workload that shows why he calls triathlon “probably one of the hardest sports out there”.
As daunted as I felt, Andrew reassured me: “I want to give you a taste of how elite athletes train. And I want you to fall in love with our sport so you keep coming back for more.”
He condensed the routine into a scaled-down version of the elite morning set: three rounds of a 100m swim followed by five minutes on the bike. The goal, he said, was to teach endurance, discipline and control.
After the first round, I was completely gassed. I had not been swimming for a while, and climbing out of the pool left me breathless.
He reminded me that consistency was the key to endurance – and that his job often involved motivating athletes to slow down.
“If you slow down and finish sessions day after day, week after week, then that’s where you really begin to see the improvements,” he said.
After the second round, I started feeling the burn in my quads from kicking too hard. I was struggling, but he kept me focused on discipline.
“Harder isn’t better. You don’t want to finish the session in a hole,” he added.
After the final round, Andrew performed an eye-opening blood lactate test to gauge my fitness level.
Lactate is produced during intense exercise when the body needs to generate energy quickly without oxygen – in other words, it is a key indicator of how hard the body is working.

While elite athletes typically record a lactate concentration of 3 to 4 mmol/L after a long, hard session – as they are trained to clear it efficiently – my result was a conclusive 16 mmol/L.
That high reading, a direct reflection of my lack of conditioning in these disciplines, was a humbling lesson.
My training day was not over yet. After a short recovery, I joined members of the Hong Kong team for running drills. Even after my scaled-down effort, I ended the day thoroughly exhausted but content.
I left the institute with immense respect for those dedicated to the sport, who train with precision and patience day after day.
The key takeaway? Triathlon is something almost anyone can take up.
As Andrew emphasised, the most important thing is consistency.
“You don’t need to do anything hard, anything specific, anything complicated. You just need to get out there and get some steps in basically or some lengths in the pool,” he said.

data.title: Andrew’s loss of ‘prince’ title echoes downfall of many a demoted royal in imperial China
data.description:
“The Andrew formerly known as Prince,” British tabloid The Sun announced on its front page on October 31. It was the cheekiest headline I have seen in a while.
Once a senior working royal with honorary military roles and public duties, Andrew, the younger brother of King Charles, was stripped of his patronages and barred from using “His Royal Highness” in 2022 after public outrage over his ties to child sex offender Jeffrey Epstein, and a civil settlement with Virginia Giuffre, who accused him of sexually abusing her when she was a teenager.
Last month, following the posthumous publication of Giuffre’s memoir six months after her suicide, Charles moved to strip Andrew of his princely title entirely. Henceforth, he will be known simply as “Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor”.
Though still technically in the line of succession, he has been reduced, in effect, to commoner status and exiled from public life.
In imperial China, princes of the blood did not often get demoted to commoner status, but when it happened, it usually followed palace intrigue or succession tussles. When a prince was perceived as a threat to their power, fathers, brothers, grandfathers and even mothers were ruthless in taking down their own flesh and blood.

Once stripped of rank, a prince’s fall was absolute. He lost his title and all the privileges that came with it. Without a state stipend, he could slip into poverty if he did not have a private income, like land or property.
However exalted his lineage, demotion from prince to commoner stripped him of his place in the imperial clan. His name was expunged from the clan register, severing him from the political influence and social prestige that came with belonging to the empire’s most exclusive circle.
His immediate family would not escape the fallout. Once a princely lineage fell, it usually stayed down. His children, grandchildren and their descendants could end up ordinary taxpayers, living among common folk for generations, boasting about their illustrious ancestor to anyone who would listen.
Humiliation and poverty were indignities to be suffered for sure, but the constant fear in which he lived his diminished life was the greater torment. His demotion to commoner status was often just the beginning of more terrible things to come.
The ex-prince would be constantly watched, whether he remained in the capital or was exiled to a distant corner of the realm, because his imperial bloodline would always be a potential political hazard. Even if he had no intention or ability to rebel, he could be a rallying point for insurgents to challenge the reigning emperor or whoever was in power.

His visitors and correspondence would be monitored, if they were not restricted altogether, to pre-empt any subversive plots. He might be confined within his quarters.
If it was decided that demotion, house arrest or even exile was insufficient to remove the political danger that the former prince engendered, there was always the ultimate option – death.
When the emperor, or the person acting in his name, “bestowed the gift of death” on an ex-prince, the latter was usually given the choice to take his own life, usually by taking poison or hanging himself. A refusal could bring swift execution by poison, strangulation or the sword.
Thankfully, such grim measures are no longer necessary, and Andrew Mountbatten-Windsor can live out his days in some comfort, albeit ignominiously, with financial support from Charles.
Yet in an age that prizes merit over bloodlines, at least in theory, the spectacle of an entire nation in a tizzy over a disgraced prince feels almost comic. If anything, Andrew’s fall reminds us that people like him are neither inherently superior nor preordained to demand our deference. They are royal only if we allow them to be.

data.title: Hong Kong triathletes ‘in great shape’, aim to turn National Games pressure into a positive
data.description:
Andrew Wright has conjured plans that give his triathletes the best chance of delivering results for Hong Kong, but the head coach acknowledged he was feeling the heat ahead of the marquee Central Harbourfront National Games races this weekend.
The city squad have three men and three women competing in Saturday’s individual events, before six are trimmed to four for the mixed relay on Sunday.
Wright said the men’s and relay races provided an equal chance of medals for Hong Kong, adding: “We know how the provinces are training and we’ve seen them in races, we’re very comparable to the top athletes there.”
Although he had no qualms about talking up his athletes’ podium prospects, Wright said he was “100 per cent” feeling the pressure.
“Acknowledging that and admitting it gives them confidence that they’re not in it on their own, I’m s******* myself, too, going into these things,” he said.
As anticipation heightened towards competition day, Wright was constantly reminding the athletes that pressure was a positive thing that “helps you perform rather than crushes you”.
“You have to get ahead of it, acknowledge it and reframe it,” he said.
“For some, this might be their last or only major Games, so I’m telling them to get some enjoyment out of it. If you can enjoy the whole process, you will perform better.”

All six of Wright’s squad were visibly in fabulous condition when they spoke to the media last week, following an intense five-week altitude camp in Yunnan.
Their chances of emerging from the individual competitions with something tangible to show for their efforts potentially rest on the shoulders of the gun men’s duo of Oscar Coggins and Robin Elg, who were fourth and fifth, respectively, in August’s Asia Triathlon Championships.
The leading women’s performer, Bailee Brown, has recovered her fitness and natural ebullience after a nine-month injury ordeal.
“Every person is as important as the next one, but having your strongest team on race day, which I believe we have, is super important,” Wright said. “I’m so happy everyone is fit and ready to go.”
Wright accepted that it was very important the triathletes performed on home ground, because “it would keep the spotlight on the sport, keep people interested and drive participation, which is really lacking in Hong Kong”.
He also believed that greater scrutiny fed into increased accountability and better performances.
The plan Wright’s men have been asked to execute on Saturday involved a blistering start that would cash in on Coggins and Elg’s swimming and biking prowess, and minimised the influence of their weaker running suit. Wong Tsz-to’s strength lay in his foot speed, so his task was to avoid becoming detached before the closing run.

Brown, Cade Wright and Hilda Choi Yan-yin have worked furiously on their swims to ensure they were part of the race as it began to play to their biking and running specialities.
“All we’re asking is that they do their best to deliver their race strategies,” Wright said.
“Something’s clicked over the past six months; they’ve trained at their highest level and conducted themselves professionally. They’re all in great shape.”
When the talking stops and the action starts, on a technical course that Wright said gave his athletes home advantage, the coach is powerless to do anything other than “sit tight”.
“Once the athletes go, a lot of coaches pretend they’re doing something, but they’re not,” Wright said.
“If you get an opportunity to give a bit of motivation, you try, but most of the time they can’t hear you.”
